Name: REQ-LLD-001
Content: When a user inputs physical digits, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall invoke `client.verifyPINFormat()` to validate the length before execution.
Rationale: Validates basic numeric formatting rules locally at the interface edge layer.

Name: REQ-LLD-002
Content: When `client.verifyPINFormat()` detects an invalid length, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall display an error message within 500 milliseconds.
Rationale: Guarantees immediate UI feedback loop when string conditions are violated.

Name: REQ-LLD-003
Content: Where an encrypted handshake is initiated, the [ATM Controller] shall execute `middleware.router.routeRequest()` within 100 milliseconds.
Rationale: Enforces crisp execution times within the core message routing layer.

Name: REQ-LLD-004
Content: When `middleware.router.routeRequest()` executes, the User Session State Handler shall extract the `session_id` from the payload buffer.
Rationale: Ensures precise parsing of the identification token from incoming packets.

Name: REQ-LLD-005
Content: When the user selects a withdrawal transaction, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall package the `withdrawal_amount` into the payload structure.
Rationale: Guarantees the financial variable is properly bound inside the client message.

Name: REQ-LLD-006
Content: If the network connection drops during a withdrawal selection, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall abort data transmission.
Rationale: Safely prevents dangling network sockets when physical links fail during use.

Name: REQ-LLD-007
Content: If the [ATM Controller] transmits a Ledger Balance Query, the [CBS Bank Host] shall invoke `core.checkLedgerBalance()` within 400 milliseconds.
Rationale: Enforces low latency constraints on the core database query methods.

Name: REQ-LLD-008
Content: When `core.checkLedgerBalance()` returns a query timeout, the [CBS Bank Host] shall broadcast a null balance token to the middleware router.
Rationale: Standardizes system behavior when a database thread fails to return data.

Name: REQ-LLD-009
Content: When `Core_Verify()` returns an authentication failure, the [ATM Controller] shall decrement the remaining attempt counter by exactly 1.
Rationale: Manages state logic variables safely across successive verification cycles.

Name: REQ-LLD-010
Content: If the attempt counter reaches zero during the PIN Verification Loop, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall transition to a terminal locked state.
Rationale: Prevents brute-force attempts by hard-locking the interactive UI interface.

Name: REQ-LLD-011
Content: When an AuthStatus of approved is received, the [ATM Controller] shall invoke `DispenseCash(amount: 100)` within 50 milliseconds.
Rationale: Guarantees fast, predictable delivery of the physical action command.

Name: REQ-LLD-012
Content: While the `DispenseCash` method is executing, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall toggle the physical cash shutter output relay to open.
Rationale: Coordinates low-level hardware interactions during physical currency delivery.

Name: REQ-LLD-013
Content: If the Security Gateway flags an authentication failure, the [ATM Controller] shall push the event log to the Error Handling & Logging node.
Rationale: Automates immediate local archival of unauthorized perimeter access events.

Name: REQ-LLD-014
Content: When the Error Handling & Logging node receives a failure log, it shall write the entry to non-volatile storage within 50 milliseconds.
Rationale: Guarantees audit trails are permanently captured before a system crash occurs.

Name: REQ-LLD-015
Content: When the cash is successfully dispensed, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall execute `client.dispenser.dispenseStatus()` to verify empty optics.
Rationale: Employs hardware sensors to double-check delivery before clearing states.

Name: REQ-LLD-016
Content: When `client.dispenser.dispenseStatus()` returns true, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall invoke the `TerminalUpdate()` method instantly.
Rationale: Links mechanical success flags directly to logical terminal update calls.

Name: REQ-LLD-017
Content: If the connection to the [CBS Bank Host] times out, the [ATM Controller] shall transmit an `Acknowledge(session_closed)` packet to the client.
Rationale: Orders explicit teardown packets when the back-end infrastructure is lost.

Name: REQ-LLD-018
Content: When an `Acknowledge(session_closed)` packet is parsed, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall transition to the out of service display screen.
Rationale: Modifies user-facing screens dynamically based on network teardown events.

Name: REQ-LLD-019
Content: When the User Session State Handler detects 30 seconds of user inactivity, it shall purge the active session data cache.
Rationale: Hardens defense by flushing session tokens out of volatile memory.

Name: REQ-LLD-020
Content: When a session data cache is purged due to a timeout, the [ATM Controller] shall transmit a close instruction to the active channel router.
Rationale: Propagates local session terminations down to the network routing layers.

Name: REQ-LLD-021
Content: While processing concurrent streams, the Network Message Routing Logic shall limit individual thread creation to 200 instances.
Rationale: Caps concurrent resource allocation to maintain system performance profiles.

Name: REQ-LLD-022
Content: If incoming request counts exceed 200, the Network Message Routing Logic shall redirect extra payloads to a memory queue buffer.
Rationale: Prevents heap saturation or crash events by introducing system backpressure.

Name: REQ-LLD-023
Content: When the Transaction Validation Engine identifies a malformed `encrypted_pin_block`, the [ATM Controller] shall discard the network block.
Rationale: Filters structurally invalid cryptographic elements before they reach routing.

Name: REQ-LLD-024
Content: When an `encrypted_pin_block` is discarded as corrupt, the [ATM Controller] shall append a failure trace code to the active log payload.
Rationale: Provides specific diagnostic tags for security teams investigating errors.

Name: REQ-LLD-025
Content: If the Customer Account Ledger DB is offline, the Transaction Authorization Router shall intercept all incoming `Core_Request()` calls.
Rationale: Implements a fast-fail defense at the gateway level when backends are down.

Name: REQ-LLD-026
Content: When a `Core_Request()` call is intercepted by the router, the [CBS Bank Host] shall return a direct DB_OFFLINE status code token.
Rationale: Standardizes the error signature sent upstream when storage cells fail.

Name: REQ-LLD-027
Content: When a user inputs physical keys, the Logical Human Interaction Layer shall replace numeric text with mask characters on the display.
Rationale: Obfuscates sensitive entries in the UI layer before formatting string buffers.

Name: REQ-LLD-028
Content: When mask characters are rendered on the UI, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall clear the raw character registers from memory buffers within 10 milliseconds.
Rationale: Eliminated residual human inputs from transient terminal memory structures.

Name: REQ-LLD-029
Content: When data transmission occurs, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall apply an AES-256 cipher block to the `data_payload` before socket transmission.
Rationale: Encrypts transaction message elements cleanly before they hit transit wires.

Name: REQ-LLD-030
Content: When the `data_payload` is encrypted, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall append an HMAC SHA-256 signature code to the tail of the stream block.
Rationale: Protects transaction blocks against man-in-the-middle manipulation attempts.

Name: REQ-LLD-031
Content: When a dispenser hardware error is reported, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall disable the physical cash delivery stepper motor driver.
Rationale: Cuts motor power lines instantly if mechanical feedback sensors report anomalies.

Name: REQ-LLD-032
Content: When the cash delivery stepper motor is disabled, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall transmit an active hardware failure alert code up to the middleware.
Rationale: Escalates severe mechanical line blocks to the central tracking services.

Name: REQ-LLD-033
Content: If a transaction approval token is delayed past 5 seconds, the [ATM Controller] shall invoke an internal fallback balance reversal routine.
Rationale: Maintains system balance integrity when communication windows expire mid-flight.

Name: REQ-LLD-034
Content: When the internal balance reversal routine completes, the [ATM Controller] shall log the transaction status as REVERSED in the system logs.
Rationale: Creates clean audit paths indicating when auto-reversal triggers executed.

Name: REQ-LLD-035
Content: When the user selects a withdrawal amount, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall query the local `vault_balance_current` tracking variable.
Rationale: Evaluates local currency inventory limits before routing transaction packets.

Name: REQ-LLD-036
Content: If the selected amount is greater than `vault_balance_current`, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall cancel message packaging sequences.
Rationale: Avoids generating wasteful network load when physical cash pools are dry.

Name: REQ-LLD-037
Content: Where multi-tenant message routing logic is applied, the [ATM Controller] shall partition channel identifiers into isolated memory segments.
Rationale: Enforces hard boundaries between virtual pathways to block cross-talk data leaks.

Name: REQ-LLD-038
Content: When a channel identifier is verified, the Network Message Routing Logic shall apply an explicit routing mask matching that channel ID.
Rationale: Restricts operational data pathways exclusively to verified routing paths.

Name: REQ-LLD-039
Content: When the Security Gateway identifies a fraud risk flag, the [CBS Bank Host] shall terminate the associated database transaction thread.
Rationale: Halts account balance adjustment loops instantly if fraud signatures trip.

Name: REQ-LLD-040
Content: When a database transaction thread is terminated due to fraud, the Security Gateway shall flag the account identifier status as SUSPENDED.
Rationale: Systematically protects compromised profiles against further extraction attempts.

Name: REQ-LLD-041
Content: If the terminal hardware cover switch transitions to open, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall clear its volatile encryption key registers.
Rationale: Erases cryptographic data if chassis sensors detect physical tampering.

Name: REQ-LLD-042
Content: When the encryption key registers are cleared, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall transmit a silent alert signal code to the middleware router.
Rationale: Dispatches emergency security messages to core nodes during tamper events.

Name: REQ-LLD-043
Content: When the Transaction Authorization Router receives a packet, it shall assign the packet to the `core.authorizeWithdrawal()` execution queue.
Rationale: Formally passes processing control to the core accounting framework.

Name: REQ-LLD-044
Content: When the packet is queued, the Transaction Authorization Router shall verify that execution starts within 50 milliseconds.
Rationale: Confirms the core processing pipeline satisfies internal timing rules.

Name: REQ-LLD-045
Content: Where power failure occurs at the terminal, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall redirect its processing logic to run off its onboard backup capacitors.
Rationale: Leverages short-term physical reserves to finish vital ledger updates.

Name: REQ-LLD-046
Content: While running off backup capacitors, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall copy unwritten log frames into non-volatile flash storage blocks.
Rationale: Keeps operational tracing files intact when hardware stations lose mains power.

Name: REQ-LLD-047
Content: When a user cancels during the PIN Verification Loop, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall de-energize the card capture latch mechanism.
Rationale: Releases physical locking traps so the customer can recover their payment card.

Name: REQ-LLD-048
Content: When the card capture latch mechanism de-energizes, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall pulse the card intake motor in reverse for 1500 milliseconds.
Rationale: Drives mechanical components backward to smoothly present the card to the user.

Name: REQ-LLD-049
Content: If the [ATM Controller] experiences a crash, the User Session State Handler shall reload the last cached state record from storage upon reboot.
Rationale: Reconstitutes application runtime parameters cleanly after failure events.

Name: REQ-LLD-050
Content: When the last cached state record is reloaded, the User Session State Handler shall issue an audit validation call to checking routers.
Rationale: Cross-references reloaded records with core nodes to identify mismatches.

Name: REQ-LLD-051
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] should try to run `client.verifyPINFormat()` quickly whenever a user plays with the physical inputs.
Rationale: Uses vague terms ('should try', 'quickly', 'plays with'); non-measurable.

Name: REQ-LLD-052
Content: When the PIN is entered, the client should think about format verification and handle things gracefully if it looks weird.
Rationale: Anthropomorphic ('think about'); ambiguous error condition ('looks weird').

Name: REQ-LLD-053
Content: The `middleware.router.routeRequest()` logic must execute instantly so that the user does not get annoyed waiting at the terminal.
Rationale: Non-technical performance goals ('instantly', 'does not get annoyed').

Name: REQ-LLD-054
Content: The middleware router shall handle the handshake packet perfectly and never drop any transaction data unless something bad happens.
Rationale: Unverifiable target ('perfectly'); open-ended exception ('something bad happens').

Name: REQ-LLD-055
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] shall pack up the withdrawal amount and send the payload packet to the controller in an agile manner.
Rationale: Uses buzzwords ('agile manner') which cannot be tested or measured.

Name: REQ-LLD-056
Content: If a user selects exactly $100, the client system should push that payload through the wires and hope for a quick reply token.
Rationale: Contains non-binding language ('should', 'hope for') and unmeasured speed rules.

Name: REQ-LLD-057
Content: The `core.checkLedgerBalance()` routine must look up the customer database really fast without causing any server lag at all.
Rationale: Subjective speed targets ('really fast', 'any server lag at all').

Name: REQ-LLD-058
Content: When the host gets a ledger balance query, it should run its database scripts nicely and pass data back if things are cool.
Rationale: Informal slang expressions ('nicely', 'if things are cool'); non-verifiable.

Name: REQ-LLD-059
Content: If PIN verification loop fails, the system should basically let the user try again until they get it right or get tired.
Rationale: Indefinite execution loops ('until they get it right or get tired'); highly ambiguous.

Name: REQ-LLD-060
Content: The loop module must verify PIN codes and retry transactions and block bad users and display nice screens all at once.
Rationale: Combines multiple distinct tasks into one requirement; violates EARS simplicity.

Name: REQ-LLD-061
Content: When `DispenseCash` is called, cash money should fly out of the terminal vault shutter safely and quickly every single time.
Rationale: Unprofessional terminology ('cash money should fly out') and unmeasurable targets.

Name: REQ-LLD-062
Content: If an approval token shows up at the terminal dispenser, it must process the cash dispensing thing like we talked about in the meeting.
Rationale: Circular reference back to informal outside sources ('like we talked about in the meeting').

Name: REQ-LLD-063
Content: The Error Handling & Logging block should catch gateway errors and write them somewhere safe without taking up too much disk room.
Rationale: Vague locations and metrics ('somewhere safe', 'too much disk room').

Name: REQ-LLD-064
Content: Where security gateway logs are generated, the logging database must save them beautifully so engineers can read them easily.
Rationale: Purely subjective design expectations ('beautifully', 'easily').

Name: REQ-LLD-065
Content: When the cash is gone, `client.dispenser.dispenseStatus()` needs to update the controller state quickly before the user walks away.
Rationale: Vague time boundaries dependent on external user behavior ('before the user walks away').

Name: REQ-LLD-066
Content: The terminal state update packet must be sent to the middleware loop so that management can track terminal metrics over the web.
Rationale: Outlines business objectives rather than describing technical software behavior.

Name: REQ-LLD-067
Content: If the host connection times out, the middleware should try its best to fix the line or pop up an error message eventually.
Rationale: Vague and indefinite recovery timeframes ('try its best', 'eventually').

Name: REQ-LLD-068
Content: When a core timeout token happens, the controller should throw a fit and tell the terminal that things are broken.
Rationale: Uses highly unprofessional, non-technical slang ('throw a fit', 'things are broken').

Name: REQ-LLD-069
Content: The User Session State Handler should destroy data pools when the user goes idle for a little bit to block sneaky hackers.
Rationale: Unquantified timeline metrics ('for a little bit') and emotional language ('sneaky hackers').

Name: REQ-LLD-070
Content: When session timeouts trigger, the system must clean up active variables so the machine looks fresh for the next person using it.
Rationale: Completely subjective performance conditions ('looks fresh').

Name: REQ-LLD-071
Content: The routing logic must handle a ton of requests at the same time without showing any signs of system stress or slowdowns.
Rationale: Non-measurable scalability parameters ('a ton', 'signs of system stress').

Name: REQ-LLD-072
Content: While routing logic is dealing with massive traffic, it should execute code flawlessly like modern high-end software.
Rationale: Meaningless comparison statements ('flawlessly', 'like modern high-end software').

Name: REQ-LLD-073
Content: If the validation engine sees a messed up payload block, it should drop it like a hot potato and throw a massive error code.
Rationale: Uses unprofessional expressions ('drop it like a hot potato', 'massive error code').

Name: REQ-LLD-074
Content: When corrupted data payloads arrive at the middleware layer, the system should handle them safely without exploding the application.
Rationale: Highly ambiguous safety guidelines ('safely', 'without exploding the application').

Name: REQ-LLD-075
Content: If the ledger database goes to sleep, the host router should tell everyone that requests are totally blocked for now.
Rationale: Ambiguous state names and scopes ('goes to sleep', 'tell everyone', 'totally blocked').

Name: REQ-LLD-076
Content: When account databases fail, the system must drop core requests beautifully so the network backend does not get ruined.
Rationale: Vague technical risk parameters ('beautifully', 'does not get ruined').

Name: REQ-LLD-077
Content: The display screen must hide user key entries with stars or dots so that bad people behind them can't see the secret numbers.
Rationale: Emotional, non-technical phrasing ('bad people', 'secret numbers').

Name: REQ-LLD-078
Content: When physical keys are clicked, the UI needs to react super fast so the user knows their key input actually worked fine.
Rationale: Lacks specific numeric timing limits ('super fast', 'worked fine').

Name: REQ-LLD-079
Content: The terminal application must scramble the transaction payload with AES code so well that no one can decrypt it ever.
Rationale: Vague crypto capability targets ('scramble... so well', 'no one can decrypt it ever').

Name: REQ-LLD-080
Content: Where payloads flow across wires, they should be encrypted using super modern tools that match global corporate standards.
Rationale: Meaningless marketing buzzwords ('super modern tools', 'global corporate standards').

Name: REQ-LLD-081
Content: If the cash machine gets jammed up, the terminal software should think about stopping before it breaks more plastic gears.
Rationale: Non-verifiable anthropomorphic instruction ('think about stopping', 'breaks more plastic gears').

Name: REQ-LLD-082
Content: When dispensing hardware throws an alert, the local interface loop should stop running things until a repair technician fixes it.
Rationale: Unbounded operational block timeframe ('until a repair technician fixes it').

Name: REQ-LLD-083
Content: If an approval token takes forever to get to the controller, the middleware should run an auto-reversal scheme automatically.
Rationale: Non-measurable boundary definition ('takes forever').

Name: REQ-LLD-084
Content: When token delays happen inside the middleware application, the system should reverse ledger changes nicely to avoid complaints.
Rationale: Subjective risk framework statement ('nicely to avoid complaints').

Name: REQ-LLD-085
Content: When a customer wants a lot of cash, the terminal must quickly check if the vault box has enough money left inside it.
Rationale: Ambiguous parameters with no numeric constraints ('a lot of cash', 'quickly check').

Name: REQ-LLD-086
Content: If the user requests more money than the vault holds, the screen must display an insufficient funds alert in a nice bold font.
Rationale: Focuses on aesthetic attributes ('nice bold font') instead of structural logic.

Name: REQ-LLD-087
Content: The middleware application should isolate tenant data structures so perfectly that cross-site leakage is completely impossible.
Rationale: Idealistic requirement containing an unachievable target ('completely impossible').

Name: REQ-LLD-088
Content: Where multi-tenant message routing logic is running, it must separate data lines clearly so info does not mix up weirdly.
Rationale: Highly informal and imprecise operational description ('mix up weirdly').

Name: REQ-LLD-089
Content: When fraud flags are raised by the gateway, the host bank system should freeze everything instantly to protect user funds.
Rationale: Unquantified scope block commands ('freeze everything instantly').

Name: REQ-LLD-090
Content: If a bad transaction signature is caught by security routers, the system must block it fast before any money is lost.
Rationale: Vague timelines and emotional targets ('block it fast', 'before any money is lost').

Name: REQ-LLD-091
Content: If the terminal box is cracked open by bad guys, the local computer must delete its keys before the bad guys steal them.
Rationale: Non-technical security description utilizing slang terms ('cracked open by bad guys').

Name: REQ-LLD-092
Content: When structural tamper alerts trigger at the terminal edge layer, state messages should fly up to the central cloud tracker.
Rationale: Highly informal phrasing ('state messages should fly up') lacking architectural precision.

Name: REQ-LLD-093
Content: The Transaction Authorization Router should send packets down to the account ledger database as fast as humanly possible.
Rationale: Immeasurable performance milestone metric ('as fast as humanly possible').

Name: REQ-LLD-094
Content: When a core request is captured by routing modules, it should hit the ledger DB database layer within a split second.
Rationale: Uses colloquial timing expressions ('within a split second') instead of clean millisecond bounds.

Name: REQ-LLD-095
Content: If the terminal wall socket power goes dead, the system should save everything to memory and hope it boots up fine later.
Rationale: Highly casual, non-falsifiable expectation language ('hope it boots up fine later').

Name: REQ-LLD-096
Content: Where brownouts happen at the physical station, the terminal should run on backup power long enough to make sure logs look good.
Rationale: Intentionally subjective evaluation metric ('long enough to make sure logs look good').

Name: REQ-LLD-097
Content: When a user hits the cancel button during PIN checks, the card slot mechanism should unlock immediately without getting jammed.
Rationale: Outlines a mechanical wish ('without getting jammed') rather than verified low-level software actions.

Name: REQ-LLD-098
Content: If the user gets scared and cancels the transaction loop, the terminal system should spit their card back out gracefully.
Rationale: Anthropomorphic slang phrasing ('gets scared', 'spit their card back out gracefully').

Name: REQ-LLD-099
Content: When the controller application reboots after a bad crash, it must fix its broken memory states magically and start working again.
Rationale: Unfalsifiable execution path instructions ('fix its broken memory states magically').

Name: REQ-LLD-100
Content: The controller should make sure everything is okay when it wakes up from a crash so data loops look totally fine.
Rationale: Very vague parameters ('everything is okay', 'totally fine').

Name: REQ-LLD-101
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] internal card reader hardware driver shall parse ISO 7816 track data formats within 200 milliseconds of card insertion.
Rationale: Governs isolated physical reader protocols completely separate from top-level system paths.

Name: REQ-LLD-102
Content: When track data parsing completes, the [ATM Client Terminal] shall copy the primary account number (PAN) to local volatile RAM segment B1.
Rationale: Allocates isolated memory pointers for raw peripheral string capture data.

Name: REQ-LLD-103
Content: If the parsed card track data fails a Longitudinal Redundancy Check (LRC), the [ATM Client Terminal] shall flag the card format as corrupted.
Rationale: Enforces checksum rules on individual magnetic card stripe reading loops.

Name: REQ-LLD-104
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] shall execute a dynamic display refresh loop exactly every 16 milliseconds to maintain a 60Hz UI frame rate.
Rationale: Dictates local graphical hardware panel driver clock rates independently of backend paths.

Name: REQ-LLD-105
Content: While rendering font characters on the terminal screen, the LCD driver module shall apply anti-aliasing filtering curves automatically.
Rationale: Handles standalone edge-layer styling parameters without reference to transaction data.

Name: REQ-LLD-106
Content: When a user touches the capacitive screen panel, the sensor interface driver shall calculate the raw pixel coordinate point within 5 milliseconds.
Rationale: Measures isolated hardware controller performance bounds during touch input captures.

Name: REQ-LLD-107
Content: If the capacitive touch coordinate falls outside the active UI button bounding box, the touch input shall be discarded by the client.
Rationale: Standardizes hit-box verification logic for interactive user interface panels.

Name: REQ-LLD-108
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] local keypad controller shall execute an internal de-bounce delay loop of exactly 20 milliseconds per key depression.
Rationale: Implements standalone electrical noise protection filters directly on physical key pins.

Name: REQ-LLD-109
Content: When a physical keypad button is released, the keypad encoder shall clear its internal interrupt request (IRQ) latch instantly.
Rationale: Clears low-level register pins cleanly to allow subsequent keyboard scans.

Name: REQ-LLD-110
Content: The [ATM Controller] internal file system shall rotate trace log files as soon as an individual log file allocation size hits 10MB.
Rationale: Manages local storage allocations on middleware partitions independently of system streams.

Name: REQ-LLD-111
Content: When log file rotation occurs, the middleware application shall compress the old text ledger file into a standard GZIP format block.
Rationale: Automates local space-saving compression routines on internal diagnostic arrays.

Name: REQ-LLD-112
Content: If the middleware log partition drops below 15% free space, the [ATM Controller] shall delete the oldest compressed log archive files.
Rationale: Prevents file system lockouts by running automated housekeeping scripts on storage.

Name: REQ-LLD-113
Content: The [ATM Controller] memory allocation pool shall pre-allocate 512KB of static RAM for each connection thread during initialization.
Rationale: Hardcodes static memory allocation targets to block out-of-memory errors under load.

Name: REQ-LLD-114
Content: When a connection thread completes execution, the [ATM Controller] garbage collector shall free the associated memory block within 10 milliseconds.
Rationale: Clears volatile pointer tracks safely to reclaim application heap capacity.

Name: REQ-LLD-115
Content: If the [ATM Controller] garbage collection loop fails to reclaim a memory pointer, the system shall flag a Leak Warning code.
Rationale: Detects residual software bugs within internal resource management routines.

Name: REQ-LLD-116
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] internal proximity sensor shall scan for physical obstacles within a 1-meter radius every 500 milliseconds.
Rationale: Operates a standalone power-saving proximity detection loop at the terminal station.

Name: REQ-LLD-117
Content: When the proximity sensor reads zero obstacles for 5 consecutive minutes, the client shall step down the LCD backlight to low-power sleep mode.
Rationale: Optimizes physical hardware component lifespan through localized power management.

Name: REQ-LLD-118
Content: When an obstacle is detected while the LCD is in sleep mode, the client shall instantly ramp up the backlight array to 100% luminance.
Rationale: Restores visibility to approaching customers using local sensor signals.

Name: REQ-LLD-119
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] physical receipt printer driver shall monitor the internal paper roll carriage feed sensor continuously.
Rationale: Tracks mechanical print supplies locally without affecting the core middleware logic.

Name: REQ-LLD-120
Content: If the paper roll feed sensor detects a paper out state, the receipt printer driver shall assert an internal PRINTER_DRY flag line.
Rationale: Notes specific binary flag changes when local printers face a lack of material.

Name: REQ-LLD-121
Content: While the PRINTER_DRY flag line is asserted, the client terminal configuration shall hide the print optional receipt checkbox from the UI.
Rationale: Dynamically alters UI layouts based on local mechanical hardware diagnostics.

Name: REQ-LLD-122
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] thermal receipt print head shall limit its local heating pulse width duration to a maximum of 1.2 milliseconds per line.
Rationale: Controls electrical print head energy limits to prevent thermal hardware burnouts.

Name: REQ-LLD-123
Content: When a print sequence terminates, the paper cutter motor relay shall trigger a full scissor sweep across the paper lane within 100 milliseconds.
Rationale: Coordinates physical cutting gear timing after text printing finishes.

Name: REQ-LLD-124
Content: The [ATM Controller] internal network interface card driver shall process TCP socket connection keep-alive pings exactly every 10 seconds.
Rationale: Runs independent network heartbeats to verify basic point-to-point connection integrity.

Name: REQ-LLD-125
Content: If a keep-alive ping response is not captured within 3 seconds, the network driver shall flag the socket as DISCONNECTED.
Rationale: Marks dead network paths quickly at the operating system driver level.

Name: REQ-LLD-126
Content: When a network socket flags a DISCONNECTED state, the network driver shall initiate an auto-reconnect sequence up to 5 times.
Rationale: Drives automated socket recovery loops at the network subsystem level.

Name: REQ-LLD-127
Content: The [ATM Controller] application shall compute a 32-bit Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC32) across all configuration text profiles on startup.
Rationale: Verifies local configuration file integrity before initializing the application.

Name: REQ-LLD-128
Content: If the computed configuration CRC32 value mismatches the stored master hash, the controller shall halt execution instantly.
Rationale: Prevents booting with corrupted or unsafely altered parameter files.

Name: REQ-LLD-129
Content: The [CBS Bank Host] database engine shall index incoming ledger records using a balanced B-Tree schema structured on the account ID key.
Rationale: Organizes raw database storage blocks to optimize micro-level index lookup tracking.

Name: REQ-LLD-130
Content: When a B-Tree node split occurs during indexing, the database engine shall update adjacent node pointers within 15 milliseconds.
Rationale: Prevents indexing corruption by forcing quick rebalancing updates in storage.

Name: REQ-LLD-131
Content: The [CBS Bank Host] internal hardware clock module shall sync its timestamp with a stratum 1 NTP server once every 60 minutes.
Rationale: Synchronizes system-level clock chips with absolute UTC references for logging accuracy.

Name: REQ-LLD-132
Content: If the NTP server clock drift calculation exceeds 50 milliseconds, the host clock module shall adjust its system tick rate by 1% until synced.
Rationale: Applies gradual clock skew corrections to protect system timestamp sequencing.

Name: REQ-LLD-133
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] audio speaker amplifier circuit shall clip output voltage levels at 2.5V RMS to prevent driver blowouts.
Rationale: Enforces electronic safety clips directly on analog audio hardware loops.

Name: REQ-LLD-134
Content: When the user interface advances to a new screen layout, the terminal shall invoke a short beep audio file lasting exactly 100 milliseconds.
Rationale: Drives minor interactive audio cues entirely inside the local client environment.

Name: REQ-LLD-135
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] internal climate monitoring controller shall turn on the internal heating element if temperatures drop below 5°C.
Rationale: Governs localized station environmental controls to preserve physical system stability.

Name: REQ-LLD-136
Content: When internal terminal temperatures exceed 45°C, the climate controller shall activate the chassis exhaust fan array at maximum speed.
Rationale: Prevents localized computing hardware failures by managing terminal airflow.

Name: REQ-LLD-137
Content: The [ATM Controller] shall deploy an asymmetric RSA-4096 cryptographic key exchange loop whenever a new client terminal connects.
Rationale: Sets up specific handshake protocol keys at the middleware socket initialization layer.

Name: REQ-LLD-138
Content: When an RSA-4096 key pair is generated, the controller shall write the public key string to a volatile RAM buffer block.
Rationale: Allocates transient cryptographic key vectors to keep network handshakes secure.

Name: REQ-LLD-139
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] physical card mouth bezel LED light ring shall pulse at a frequency of 1Hz while waiting for a card.
Rationale: Controls localized cosmetic indicators on the physical terminal facade.

Name: REQ-LLD-140
Content: When a card is successfully swallowed by the card slot, the bezel LED light ring shall shift to a solid illuminated state.
Rationale: Modifies physical light signatures based on basic entry sensor updates.

Name: REQ-LLD-141
Content: The [CBS Bank Host] security gateway shall store active blocklisted IP ranges in a localized high-speed Radix tree data structure.
Rationale: Structures network filter sets inside dedicated data frameworks to allow fast lookups.

Name: REQ-LLD-142
Content: When a packet is parsed by the security gateway, its source IP shall be checked against the Radix tree structure within 2 milliseconds.
Rationale: Sets rigorous processing times for edge firewall checks.

Name: REQ-LLD-143
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] local vault door sensor shall read the continuity status of the physical vault frame loop wire every 10 milliseconds.
Rationale: Monitors low-level safety circuitry on the terminal vault door.

Name: REQ-LLD-144
Content: If the continuity status of the vault loop wire breaks, the terminal shall latch its electronic security alarm pin to an active-high state.
Rationale: Triggers hardwired safety alarms if structural break-in attempts damage system components.

Name: REQ-LLD-145
Content: The [ATM Controller] diagnostic subsystem shall execute an automated RAM test across unallocated memory blocks every 24 hours.
Rationale: Periodically checks for hardware degradation inside internal middleware memory cells.

Name: REQ-LLD-146
Content: If the automated RAM test encounters a single bit-flip error, the controller shall dispatch a maintenance request ticket flag.
Rationale: Identifies early physical memory damage before it causes software instability.

Name: REQ-LLD-147
Content: The [ATM Client Terminal] alphanumeric thermal receipt printer font set shall be loaded from an integrated onboard EEPROM chip on boot.
Rationale: Initializes localized character sets directly from hardware memory vectors.

Name: REQ-LLD-148
Content: When the terminal font set loads, the printer driver shall cross-reference character widths to ensure font scale conformity.
Rationale: Validates visual rendering variables before permitting receipt print sequences.

Name: REQ-LLD-149
Content: The [CBS Bank Host] transaction router shall use an internal round-robin balancing scheme to distribute database queries across server clusters.
Rationale: Dictates isolated server balancing patterns completely separate from terminal processes.

Name: REQ-LLD-150
Content: When a new database server cluster node enters an active state, the router shall include the node identifier in the round-robin loop within 1 second.
Rationale: Dynamically scales available resources within the core infrastructure.

Name: REQ-LLD-151
Content: The card reader hardware driver should read ISO cards super fast and work nicely with modern plastic bank card profiles.
Rationale: Vague speed metrics ('super fast', 'work nicely'); non-verifiable.

Name: REQ-LLD-152
Content: When track data is parsed, the system should dump it somewhere in RAM and hope it does not get mixed up with other data lines.
Rationale: Imprecise memory planning ('dump it somewhere', 'hope it does not get mixed up').

Name: REQ-LLD-153
Content: The terminal screen update must look super smooth and refresh so fast that the human eye cannot see any screen flickering at all.
Rationale: Highly subjective visual metric targets ('super smooth', 'so fast that the human eye cannot see').

Name: REQ-LLD-154
Content: The LCD display driver should show cool graphics and use high-end rendering styles like a video game console.
Rationale: Contains non-binding wishful terminology and informal comparisons ('cool graphics', 'like a video game').

Name: REQ-LLD-155
Content: When a customer clicks the screen, the system should pick up the touch coordinates instantly without being laggy.
Rationale: Lacks quantified engineering metrics ('instantly', 'without being laggy').

Name: REQ-LLD-156
Content: Touch targets inside the terminal interface should be big enough so that users with thick fingers don't tap the wrong button.
Rationale: Imprecise structural metrics based on external human traits ('thick fingers').

Name: REQ-LLD-157
Content: The terminal keyboard hardware driver must clean up electrical bounce signals nicely so everything feels crisp to the touch.
Rationale: Subjective haptic feel targets ('feels crisp to the touch', 'nicely').

Name: REQ-LLD-158
Content: When keys are pressed down, the system should not lock up or act weird if the user bashes multiple buttons at once.
Rationale: Highly informal slang terms ('act weird', 'bashes multiple buttons').

Name: REQ-LLD-159
Content: The internal log files on the middleware server should clean themselves up periodically so the drive does not get super full.
Rationale: Non-measurable storage boundaries ('clean themselves up', 'super full').

Name: REQ-LLD-160
Content: When log compression runs, the system should use a compression tool that works beautifully and never corrupts zip text files.
Rationale: Subjective capability markers ('works beautifully', 'never corrupts').

Name: REQ-LLD-161
Content: If space gets low on the middleware disk, the system should think about wiping out old logs that nobody care about anymore.
Rationale: Anthropomorphic system behavior instruction ('think about wiping out', 'nobody care about').

Name: REQ-LLD-162
Content: The middleware memory buffer must allocate an absolute ton of RAM so connection processing threads never feel starved.
Rationale: Vague capability targets ('an absolute ton', 'never feel starved').

Name: REQ-LLD-163
Content: The garbage collection software should run in the background and clean up dead memory blocks loops like magic.
Rationale: Uses non-falsifiable performance adjectives ('like magic').

Name: REQ-LLD-164
Content: If a memory pointer leak happens, the app should fix itself automatically without bugging the main system developers.
Rationale: Lacks formal error handling or explicit recovery vectors ('fix itself automatically').

Name: REQ-LLD-165
Content: The local proximity radar must check for people hanging around the ATM machine and make sure they are not creepy.
Rationale: Uses emotional and unmeasurable criteria ('creepy', 'hanging around').

Name: REQ-LLD-166
Content: When nobody is near the terminal booth, the monitor should turn down its lights to save cash on the electricity bill.
Rationale: Explains operational business motives instead of explicit technical engineering triggers.

Name: REQ-LLD-167
Content: If someone walks up to the kiosk, the display screen should wake up instantly and look incredibly bright and inviting.
Rationale: Purely aesthetic design language ('incredibly bright and inviting', 'instantly').

Name: REQ-LLD-168
Content: The printer roll monitor should check if paper is left inside the slot and flash a warning if it looks empty.
Rationale: Ambiguous validation parameters ('flash a warning if it looks empty').

Name: REQ-LLD-169
Content: If the receipt paper is jammed up, the interface should stop offering printouts and guide the customer nicely.
Rationale: Subjective user experience guidelines ('guide the customer nicely').

Name: REQ-LLD-170
Content: The receipt print head must not get super hot or burn the paper if it prints out a really long receipt frame.
Rationale: Vague environmental boundaries ('super hot', 'a really long receipt frame').

Name: REQ-LLD-171
Content: When the paper is printed, the scissor blade motor should chop the slip off quickly without making too much noise.
Rationale: Non-measurable mechanical acoustics constraint ('without making too much noise').

Name: REQ-LLD-172
Content: The controller network stack must send keep-alive pings down the wire and ensure the line is fast and crisp.
Rationale: Indefinite network connection parameters ('fast and crisp').

Name: REQ-LLD-173
Content: If connection pings drop out, the driver should keep hammering the socket port forever until things come back alive.
Rationale: Creates a dangerous infinite block state condition ('hammering the socket port forever').

Name: REQ-LLD-174
Content: The controller startup process should check its setup text files and make sure management didn't put bad configurations inside.
Rationale: Informal organizational reference phrasing ('make sure management didn't put bad configs').

Name: REQ-LLD-175
Content: If configuration profiles look weird on startup, the application should crash gracefully and display a cool error screen.
Rationale: Ambiguous logic filters and informal adjectives ('look weird', 'cool error screen').

Name: REQ-LLD-176
Content: The core backend database must structure its indices beautifully using modern trees so lookup scripts run instantly.
Rationale: Lacks precise software performance timings ('beautifully', 'instantly').

Name: REQ-LLD-177
Content: When database indices rebalance themselves, the server mainframe should not choke up or drop ongoing transactions.
Rationale: Highly unprofessional slang expressions ('choke up').

Name: REQ-LLD-178
Content: The bank system master clock needs to talk to standard server clocks online so timestamps don't get out of sync.
Rationale: Imprecise external data references ('standard server clocks online'); lacks exact timing metrics.

Name: REQ-LLD-179
Content: If the internal host clock drifts away, it should recalibrate itself quietly without causing any data anomalies.
Rationale: Unquantified validation parameters ('quietly', 'any data anomalies').

Name: REQ-LLD-180
Content: The terminal audio speaker system must sound clear and loud enough so old people can hear the confirmation beeps easily.
Rationale: Imprecise demographic target references ('old people', 'loud enough'); non-verifiable.

Name: REQ-LLD-181
Content: When a user hits a button, a cute confirmation chime should play through the speakers without sounding distorted.
Rationale: Subjective aesthetic audio properties ('cute confirmation chime', 'without sounding distorted').

Name: REQ-LLD-182
Content: The internal air conditioner switch should turn on fans when the cabinet gets super hot inside so circuits do not melt.
Rationale: Dramatic, non-technical risk descriptors ('super hot', 'so circuits do not melt').

Name: REQ-LLD-183
Content: If the kiosk temperature goes below freezing, a heater should kick in fast to keep the computer boards warm and happy.
Rationale: Anthropomorphic system design goal language ('keep the computer boards warm and happy').

Name: REQ-LLD-184
Content: The controller application should deploy heavy cryptographic math when building connection sockets so hackers get confused.
Rationale: Emotional target definitions ('heavy cryptographic math', 'so hackers get confused').

Name: REQ-LLD-185
Content: When cryptographic public keys are built by the middleware layer, they should be stored safely in a super secure spot.
Rationale: Vague storage location definitions ('stored safely in a super secure spot').

Name: REQ-LLD-186
Content: The LED ring around the card reader mouth should blink happily to attract customers walking down the street.
Rationale: Anthropomorphic description focusing on external marketing goals ('blink happily to attract customers').

Name: REQ-LLD-187
Content: When a payment card gets stuck in the reader, the LED slot light must flash red like crazy to show something went wrong.
Rationale: Highly informal and imprecise indicator description ('flash red like crazy').

Name: REQ-LLD-188
Content: The host blocklist tree data setup must hold millions of bad IP numbers and look them up super fast without stalling.
Rationale: Uses vague metrics and unverified classifications ('millions of bad IP numbers', 'super fast').

Name: REQ-LLD-189
Content: When a client packet hits the security wall, the gateway should double-check its source path against the blocklist nicely.
Rationale: Intentionally subjective execution description ('double-check... nicely').

Name: REQ-LLD-190
Content: The terminal vault door sensor circuit should be robust and catch bad guys trying to break open the cash vault box.
Rationale: Non-technical security description using informal slang terms ('catch bad guys').

Name: REQ-LLD-191
Content: If the security wire on the cash vault frame is sliced, the terminal should scream an alarm loud enough to scare thieves away.
Rationale: Highly emotional and non-falsifiable operational targets ('scream an alarm', 'scare thieves away').

Name: REQ-LLD-192
Content: The middleware self-test routine should check system memory pools once in a while and fix broken memory cells automatically.
Rationale: Imprecise timeline intervals and missing technical recovery paths ('once in a while').

Name: REQ-LLD-193
Content: If a memory test throws a bit error, the controller system should try to heal itself or email an admin somehow.
Rationale: Highly ambiguous action directives containing non-standard escape options ('try to heal itself', 'somehow').

Name: REQ-LLD-194
Content: The printer font database array should load font vectors smoothly on boot so receipts print text effortlessly.
Rationale: Subjective aesthetic performance descriptors ('smoothly', 'effortlessly').

Name: REQ-LLD-195
Content: When character scaling arrays are read, the driver layout module must fit the text nicely onto paper slips without gaps.
Rationale: Vague structural positioning parameters ('fit the text nicely... without gaps').

Name: REQ-LLD-196
Content: The backend transaction router needs to split database workloads across host computers evenly so servers stay relaxed.
Rationale: Anthropomorphic performance tracking goals ('so servers stay relaxed').

Name: REQ-LLD-197
Content: When a database mainframe crashes, the router should move requests to another computer as fast as possible without lag.
Rationale: Immeasurable performance metrics ('as fast as possible without lag').

Name: REQ-LLD-198
Content: The card intake motor should grip plastic cards firmly but gently so it does not scratch the customer's expensive card.
Rationale: Subjective and unmeasurable physical boundaries ('firmly but gently', 'expensive card').

Name: REQ-LLD-199
Content: The terminal power backup system must watch out for power drops and keep the motherboard happy during summer blackouts.
Rationale: Informal and seasonal condition framing statements ('keep the motherboard happy during summer blackouts').

Name: REQ-LLD-200
Content: The internal climate control board should manage system fans optimally so the terminal computer never gets sweaty or overheats.
Rationale: Uses unscientific, highly unprofessional slang terms ('never gets sweaty').
